data.title: Is XP (NasdaqGS:XP) Still Offering Value After Mixed Recent Share Price Moves
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether XP at around US$19.63 is still offering value or already pricing in the story, you are not alone.
  • The stock has seen mixed moves recently, with a 7.1% decline over the past week, a 2.1% gain over 30 days, and returns of 21.3% year to date and 27.4% over the last year that may be reshaping how investors think about its risk and reward.
  • Recent news coverage around XP has largely focused on its role in Brazil's capital markets and its position in the broader financial sector. This provides context for the stock's performance over multiple time frames. Commentary has often centered on how its business model, client base, and market positioning may influence sentiment during periods of volatility.
  • Against that backdrop, XP currently records a value score of 6 out of 6. The sections that follow break down how traditional valuation methods assess the stock while hinting at a more complete way to think about value that comes at the end of this article.

XP delivered 27.4% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Capital Markets industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: XP Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to earn above the return required by its shareholders, then capitalizes those excess profits into an estimate of value per share.

For XP, the model starts with a Book Value of US$45.39 per share and an Average Return on Equity of 23.97%. On that equity base, analysts estimate Stable EPS of US$13.54 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 10 analysts. The required return, or Cost of Equity, is US$6.96 per share, which implies an Excess Return of US$6.57 per share.

Those excess profits are projected on a higher Stable Book Value of US$56.48 per share, sourced from weighted future Book Value estimates from 5 analysts. When these excess returns are capitalized, the model arrives at an intrinsic value of about US$25.99 per share for XP.

Against a current share price of about US$19.63, the Excess Returns result points to an implied discount of roughly 24.5%, which suggests the stock is undervalued on this basis.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests XP is undervalued by 24.5%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 54 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for XP.

Approach 2: XP Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like XP, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much investors are paying for each dollar of current earnings. It links directly to what the business is earning today, which many investors find easier to relate to than cash flow models or asset based measures.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on two big pieces: how quickly earnings are expected to change over time, and how risky those earnings are perceived to be. Higher growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while lower growth or higher perceived risk usually points to a lower multiple.

XP currently trades on a P/E of 9.83x. That sits below the Capital Markets industry average P/E of 41.69x and also below the peer group average of 19.01x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for XP is 14.97x, which is a proprietary estimate of what the P/E “should” be after factoring in earnings growth characteristics, profit margins, risk profile, market value and its place within the industry.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry because it adjusts for XP specific traits rather than assuming all Capital Markets firms deserve the same multiple. Set against the current 9.83x P/E, the 14.97x Fair Ratio indicates that XP’s shares are trading below this model based reference point.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your XP Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so Narratives are introduced here as your way of attaching a clear story about XP to the numbers you see, linking what you believe about its future revenue, earnings and margins to a financial forecast, an assumed fair value, and then a simple comparison with today’s share price.

On Simply Wall St’s Community page, Narratives are available as an accessible tool used by millions of investors, allowing you to set out your view in plain language, plug in assumptions, and then see how that translates into a fair value that updates automatically when new information such as news or earnings is added.

For XP, one investor might lean toward the more cautious fair value near US$22.01, based on assumptions like 12.5% annual revenue growth and a 10.2x future P/E. Another might align with a higher fair value closer to about US$27.10 that reflects stronger revenue growth expectations and a roughly 13.2x future P/E. data.title: CGI Uses OpenAI Partnerships And SOK Finance Deployment To Deepen AI Role
data.description:
• CGI (TSX:GIB.A) is expanding its AI partnership with OpenAI to deploy enterprise AI agents across client operations.
  • The company recently formed an AI focused IT partnership with steelmaker SSAB to support manufacturing processes.
  • CGI has also launched multi agent AI in SOK Finance operations, applying automated workflows to financial processes.

CGI (TSX:GIB.A) sits at the intersection of IT services, consulting and managed solutions, where AI driven automation is becoming a core part of client spending. Partnerships with OpenAI and SSAB tie the company more closely to real world use cases in manufacturing and financial services, two areas where clients are looking for productivity gains and more efficient workflows. For investors, these moves help clarify how CGI aims to position its AI offerings inside large, complex enterprises.

The rollout of multi agent AI in SOK Finance gives CGI a live reference case for automated finance operations, which can matter when competing for similar work with other clients. Readers tracking TSX:GIB.A may want to watch how these partnerships convert into additional deployments, new contract wins or extensions, and how clients describe the impact of AI agents on their day to day processes.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for CGI by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on CGI.

📰 Beyond the headline: 0 risks and 4 things going right for CGI that every investor should see.

For CGI, this news is about turning AI from a buzzword into concrete client work. Early access to OpenAI tools such as Codex, combined with deployments like SOK Finance and the expanded SSAB partnership, gives CGI more proof points across software development, finance operations and heavy manufacturing. That can matter when competing with large IT services peers such as Accenture, Capgemini or IBM for complex, multi-year contracts where clients want partners that already have AI in production, not just on slide decks. At the same time, AI agents that automate internal workflows for tens of thousands of CGI professionals may support delivery efficiency, which can help when pricing or staffing large projects.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The CGI Narrative

  • The OpenAI, SSAB and SOK Finance announcements align with the narrative theme that AI-enabled services can support demand for higher margin managed services and proprietary IP.
  • Broader AI use in areas like government and manufacturing could test assumptions about how quickly clients commit to large digital projects, especially where earlier commentary highlighted exposure to slower decision cycles.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on backlog, acquisitions and sector exposure, while these specific AI agent deployments in finance operations and steelmaking may not yet be fully reflected in longer term qualitative expectations.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for CGI to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Execution risk if AI agent deployments prove harder to scale across regions, sectors or legacy systems than early case studies suggest.
  • ⚠️ Competitive pressure from global IT firms and cloud providers that are also embedding generative AI into services, which could compress pricing or shorten contract durations.
  • 🎁 Early access to OpenAI capabilities and a multi-year SSAB partnership position CGI to pitch AI-powered solutions into both public sector and industrial clients.
  • 🎁 Live production use at SOK Finance offers a reference case for automating routine finance workflows, which CGI can point to in future bids.

What To Watch Going Forward

Investors may want to watch how often CGI cites OpenAI, SSAB and SOK Finance in future contract wins, and whether similar AI agent deployments appear in other public sector and manufacturing clients. It is also worth tracking commentary on AI-related productivity inside CGI, such as how many projects are using these tools and whether clients expand initial pilots into wider programs. Any disclosures on backlog tied specifically to AI-enabled work, or on competitive wins against firms like Accenture or IBM in AI-heavy tenders, would help show how central these initiatives are becoming to CGI's core business.

data.title: FFO Growth At Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) Tests Long Lease Stability Narrative
data.description:
Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) has kicked off Q1 2026 with total revenue of US$158.8 million, basic EPS of US$0.28 and net income of US$59.8 million, with management and investors also watching FFO of US$114.6 million and FFO per share of US$0.54 as key REIT metrics. Over the past year, the company has seen revenue move from US$129.4 million and basic EPS of US$0.30 in Q1 2025 to US$158.8 million and US$0.28 in the latest quarter, while trailing twelve month revenue reached US$590.7 million and EPS came in at US$1.27. With earnings over the last year growing 20.9% and net profit margin at 43.3%, the release provides a clear view of a business where profitability remains central to the story.

See our full analysis for Essential Properties Realty Trust.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how these results align with the widely held narratives about Essential Properties Realty Trust, and where the data pushes back against those stories.

See what the community is saying about Essential Properties Realty Trust

Advertisement

FFO and Revenue Growth Outpace EPS Trend

  • FFO rose from US$92.1 million in Q1 2025 to US$114.6 million in Q1 2026, while total revenue moved from US$129.4 million to US$158.8 million over the same periods, even as basic EPS for the latest quarter sits at US$0.28.
  • Consensus narrative points to stable, inflation-linked cash flows from long leases and service-based tenants, and the numbers back that up in some ways but not others.
    • On the supportive side, trailing 12 month revenue of US$590.7 million and earnings growth of 20.9% suggest the portfolio is still converting rent into profits at scale, in line with the focus on long leases and near full occupancy.
    • On the other hand, the step down in Q1 2026 basic EPS from US$0.30 in Q1 2025 comes alongside a net margin of 43.3% that is slightly below last year’s 44.5%, so profit per share is not moving up as clearly as the FFO and revenue lines.

Consensus viewers who want to see how this earnings mix lines up with longer term storylines can dig deeper into the community angle here 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Essential Properties Realty Trust.

Margins Stay High While Growth Moderates

  • Over the last 12 months, earnings grew 20.9% while the net profit margin sits at 43.3%, compared with 44.5% in the prior year. This 20.9% earnings growth is below the 5 year average of 24.8% per year in the dataset.
  • Consensus narrative talks about stable, predictable margin growth from inflation protected leases, and the current figures partly echo that story but also show some pressure.
    • High margins in the low 40% range fit with long leases and regular rent bumps that are expected to support steady profitability according to the narrative.
    • However, the small slip from 44.5% to 43.3%, together with forecasts that margins move lower over time, challenges the idea that profitability simply trends higher without any drag from rising expenses or sector specific risks.

Mixed Signals From Valuation and Balance Sheet

  • At a share price of US$31.50, the stock trades on a P/E of 26.6x versus peer REITs at 29.9x and a Global REITs industry average of 15.4x, while a DCF fair value of US$95.49 and an analyst price target of US$37.20 both sit above the current market level. Debt is flagged as not well covered by operating cash flow.
  • Consensus narrative highlights a strong balance sheet and funding position, and that view sits alongside a more mixed picture in the data.
    • Supportive of a constructive view, earnings growth of 20.9% and a P/E that is lower than the 29.9x peer average combine with the gap to the US$95.49 DCF fair value and US$37.20 analyst target to frame the current US$31.50 price as well below some valuation models.
    • Balancing that, the warning that debt is not well covered by operating cash flow and a P/E above the broader Global REITs industry average of 15.4x both point to financial risk and a premium pricing versus many REITs, which sits awkwardly with claims of a straightforwardly strong balance sheet.

Next Steps

data.title: It Might Not Be A Great Idea To Buy Minerva S.A. (BVMF:BEEF3) For Its Next Dividend
data.description:
It looks like Minerva S.A. (BVMF:BEEF3) is about to go ex-dividend in the next 4 days. The ex-dividend date is commonly two business days before the record date, which is the cut-off date for shareholders to be present on the company's books to be eligible for a dividend payment. It is important to be aware of the ex-dividend date because any trade on the stock needs to have been settled on or before the record date. This means that investors who purchase Minerva's shares on or after the 29th of April will not receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 13th of May.

The company's upcoming dividend is R$0.0311473 a share, following on from the last 12 months, when the company distributed a total of R$0.33 per share to shareholders. Last year's total dividend payments show that Minerva has a trailing yield of 8.2% on the current share price of R$3.99. Dividends are a major contributor to investment returns for long term holders, but only if the dividend continues to be paid. As a result, readers should always check whether Minerva has been able to grow its dividends, or if the dividend might be cut.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

If a company pays out more in dividends than it earned, then the dividend might become unsustainable - hardly an ideal situation. Minerva reported a loss after tax last year, which means it's paying a dividend despite being unprofitable. While this might be a one-off event, this is unlikely to be sustainable in the long term. Yet cash flow is typically more important than profit for assessing dividend sustainability, so we should always check if the company generated enough cash to afford its dividend. The good news is it paid out just 4.5% of its free cash flow in the last year.

View our latest analysis for Minerva

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

When earnings decline, dividend companies become much harder to analyse and own safely. If earnings decline and the company is forced to cut its dividend, investors could watch the value of their investment go up in smoke. With that in mind, we're discomforted by Minerva's 9.1% per annum decline in earnings in the past five years. When earnings per share fall, the maximum amount of dividends that can be paid also falls.

Minerva also issued more than 5% of its market cap in new stock during the past year, which we feel is likely to hurt its dividend prospects in the long run. Trying to grow the dividend while issuing large amounts of new shares reminds us of the ancient Greek tale of Sisyphus - perpetually pushing a boulder uphill.

Another key way to measure a company's dividend prospects is by measuring its historical rate of dividend growth. In the last nine years, Minerva has lifted its dividend by approximately 2.8% a year on average.

Final Takeaway

From a dividend perspective, should investors buy or avoid Minerva? It's not a great combination to see a company with earnings in decline and paying out -18% of its profits, which could imply the dividend may be at risk of being cut in the future. However, the cash payout ratio was much lower - good news from a dividend perspective - which makes us wonder why there is such a mis-match between income and cashflow. With the way things are shaping up from a dividend perspective, we'd be inclined to steer clear of Minerva.

With that in mind though, if the poor dividend characteristics of Minerva don't faze you, it's worth being mindful of the risks involved with this business. We've identified 3 warning signs with Minerva (at least 2 which are a bit concerning), and understanding these should be part of your investment process.

data.title: Boeing (BA) Q1 Loss And One Off Driven EPS Challenge Bullish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Boeing (BA) opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of US$22.2b and a basic EPS loss of US$0.11, as net income excluding extra items came in at a loss of US$90m. Over the past six reported quarters, revenue has moved from US$15.2b in Q4 2024 to a range between US$19.5b and US$24.0b through 2025. Quarterly EPS has swung between a loss of US$7.14 and a profit of US$10.59, which places greater focus on margins and reported profitability than on the headline revenue line.

See our full analysis for Boeing.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the most common narratives around Boeing, and where the data pushes back against them.

See what the community is saying about Boeing

Advertisement

Trailing 12‑month profit helped by US$9.6b one off

  • Over the latest twelve months Boeing reported net income of US$1.9b and basic EPS of US$2.50, but this outcome includes a US$9.6b one off gain that materially lifts reported profitability compared with earlier periods where trailing EPS was deeply negative.
  • What bulls highlight as a clean return to profit is more mixed in the numbers, because:
    • Trailing basic EPS moved from a loss of US$18.36 per share at Q4 2024 to a profit of US$2.50 per share at Q1 2026. That swing is heavily influenced by the one off item rather than only by operating improvements.
    • Revenue over the same trailing period grew from US$66.5b at Q4 2024 to US$92.2b at Q1 2026, which supports the bullish view of a bigger business. However, the size of the non recurring gain means current margins may not reflect a steady state.

Bulls argue the latest profit run rate points to a stronger multi year recovery story, but these figures show how much of that picture is tied to a single US$9.6b gain rather than repeatable earnings, which is exactly what the bullish case needs to clear for long term comfort. 🐂 Boeing Bull Case

High 96x P/E versus US$267.84 price target

  • Boeing currently trades on a trailing P/E of 96x, compared with an industry average of 37.7x and a peer average of 27.6x, while the share price of US$234.15 sits below the allowed analyst price target of US$267.84.
  • Consensus narrative sees this as justified by strong growth expectations, yet the data gives a mixed picture:
    • Analysts expect earnings to grow about 36.9% per year and revenue about 9.4% per year, so the current 96x multiple is being supported by forecasts that earnings will rise much faster than the top line.
    • At the same time, the DCF fair value of US$322.83 is above both the current price and the US$267.84 analyst target, which supports the optimistic view on future cash flows even as the elevated P/E looks demanding versus industry norms.

Debt coverage and one offs keep bears focused on risk

  • Risk analysis flags that operating cash flow does not currently cover debt well, while the same US$9.6b one off that lifts EPS also makes recent earnings less representative of ongoing performance.
  • Critics point to these figures as support for the cautious narrative:
    • Debt being poorly covered by operating cash flow means more of future earnings may need to go to servicing obligations, which aligns with bearish concerns about financial flexibility.
    • Significant insider selling over the past three months, alongside earnings that are materially affected by a single gain, fits the skeptical view that recent profitability and valuation multiples may not fully reflect underlying business strength.

Skeptics warn that stretched debt coverage and heavy reliance on a one time gain leave less room for error if revenue or margins soften from here. 🐻 Boeing Bear Case

data.title: Integrated Wind Solutions Q3 Net Margin Resilience Reinforces Bullish Narratives
data.description:
Advertisement

Latest FY 2025 Earnings Snapshot

Integrated Wind Solutions (OB:IWS) just posted another profitable quarter, with Q3 FY 2025 revenue of €24.1 million and basic EPS of €0.12, set against trailing twelve month revenue of €99.0 million and EPS of €0.39 that reflects very large year over year earnings growth and a margin profile now supported by a 15.6% net margin. Over recent periods the company has seen revenue move from €29.3 million with a trailing EPS loss of €0.05 in mid 2024 to €55.7 million and EPS of €0.11 by Q4 2024, and then to €99.0 million with EPS of €0.39 on a trailing basis by Q3 2025. This progression gives investors a clearer view of how revenue and EPS have built up across the last year. With that backdrop, the latest print keeps attention squarely on how sustainable these margins are rather than on any one quarter's headline surprise.

See our full analysis for Integrated Wind Solutions.

With the core numbers on the table, the next step is to set them against the most widely held narratives around Integrated Wind Solutions to see which views are reinforced and which might need a rethink.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Margins Supported by €15.5m TTM Profit

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, Integrated Wind Solutions earned €15.5 million of net income on €99.0 million of revenue, which lines up with the 15.6% net margin that has become a key part of the story.
  • What stands out for a bullish narrative is how the recent quarters stack up, with net income between €2.3 million and €4.7 million in each quarter since Q3 2024. This supports claims of higher quality earnings but also invites the question of whether this recent margin level is now the base case or still a relatively new phase.
    • Supporters can point to the move from a trailing twelve month loss of €1.9 million in mid 2024 to €4.3 million by Q4 2024 and then €15.5 million by Q3 2025 as evidence that profitability is now embedded across multiple quarters, not just a single period.
    • Skeptics may focus on how compressed the turnaround window is, because the trailing twelve month margin was 1.4% last year compared with 15.6% now. Any change in day rates or utilization would quickly show up in that margin line.

Earnings Growth and €0.39 TTM EPS

  • Trailing twelve month basic EPS sits at €0.39, built up from quarterly EPS between €0.05 and €0.12 since Q3 2024, which matches the very large year over year earnings growth figure reported over the last 12 months.
  • Consistent with a bullish view, the step from a trailing twelve month EPS loss of €0.05 in Q2 2024 to €0.11 by Q4 2024 and then €0.39 by Q3 2025 heavily supports the idea that the company has moved from loss making to a run of positive EPS. However, the range of quarterly EPS outcomes also reminds investors that this earnings base is still relatively young.
    • Bulls highlighting earnings growth of more than 2,500% over the last year can point directly to the trailing twelve month EPS path, which tracks from a small loss to €0.39 as revenue rose from €29.3 million to €99.0 million over the same window.
    • At the same time, the spread between quarterly EPS outcomes, from €0.01 in Q2 2024 to just over €0.11 in Q4 2024 and Q3 2025, shows that quarter to quarter swings are still meaningful, so using the trailing twelve month view is important when weighing the bullish thesis.

Curious how this earnings ramp fits into the bigger story investors are telling about the stock right now? See what the community is saying about Integrated Wind Solutions

Valuation Gap vs DCF Fair Value

  • With the share price at NOK56.80 against a DCF fair value of NOK188.68 and a P/E of 13.5x versus a 15.5x industry average and 24x peer average, the stock currently sits at a discount on both the DCF and P/E measures that have been flagged.
  • What is interesting for a bullish lens is how this valuation picture lines up with the profitability data, because a 15.6% trailing net margin and €15.5 million of trailing net income sit alongside a share price that is materially below the DCF fair value and below industry and peer multiples. This is the type of mismatch that value focused investors often watch for.
    • Supporters of the bullish narrative can point out that the current price is roughly 70% below the DCF fair value, while the company is reporting a run of positive net income in the €2.3 million to €4.7 million range each quarter.
    • On the other hand, the flagged risk that debt is not well covered by operating cash flow shows why some investors may want stronger cash generation to back up that margin and valuation story before feeling fully comfortable with the gap to DCF fair value.
